---
title: "The grammar of graphics and `ggplot2`"
subtitle: "<br>36-315: Statistical Graphics and Visualization"
footer:  "[36-315-summer26.netlify.app](https://36-315-summer26.netlify.app)"
format:
  revealjs:
    embed-resources: true
    height: 900
    width: 1600
    theme: theme.scss
    smaller: true
    slide-number: c/t
    linestretch: 1.4
    code-line-numbers: false
    html-math-method:
      method: mathjax
      url: "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/mathjax@3/es5/tex-mml-chtml.js"
---

```{r}
#| include: false
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center"
)
ggplot2::theme_set(ggplot2::theme_gray(base_size = 20))
```

## The data science workflow

[![Source: R for Data Science (2e)](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/diagrams/data-science/base.png){width="1100"}](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/intro)

> "The simple graph has brought more information to the data analyst’s mind than any other device." --- John Tukey

## Exploratory data analysis (EDA)

* In statistics/data science, data visualization is part of the EDA process

* Goal of EDA: perform initial explorations in order to better understand the data, discover trends/patterns, spot anomalies, etc.

. . .

* Data can be explored

  * numerically (tables, descriptive statistics,...)
  
  * **visually** (graphics; focus of this course)
  
. . .

* EDA is NOT a replacement for statistical inference and modeling

* EDA is an important and necessary step to build intuition

## ALWAYS visualize your data

**Anscombe's quartet**

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 9
library(tidyverse)
theme_set(theme_light(base_size = 20))
anscombe |> 
  pivot_longer(everything(),
               cols_vary = "slowest",
               names_to = c(".value", "set"),
               names_pattern = "(.)(.)") |> 
  ggplot(aes(x, y)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "red", fullrange = TRUE) +
  geom_point(size = 4) +
  facet_wrap(~ set)
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

<br>

```{r}
#| echo: false
anscombe |> 
  pivot_longer(everything(),
               cols_vary = "slowest",
               names_to = c(".value", "set"),
               names_pattern = "(.)(.)") |> 
  group_by(set) |> 
  summarise(across(c(x, y), list(mean = mean, var = var)),
            x_y_cor = cor(x, y)) |> 
  ungroup() |> 
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 2))) |> 
  select(panel = set, "mean(x)" = x_mean, "var(x)" = x_var, "mean(y)" = y_mean, "var(y)" = y_var, "cor(x,y)" = x_y_cor) |> 
  gt::gt() |> 
  gt::tab_options(table.font.size = "27px", data_row.padding = "18px")
```
:::
:::

## ALWAYS visualize your data

**The Datasaurus dozen: each of these has the same mean, variance, and correlation**

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 9
#| fig-width: 16
datasauRus::datasaurus_dozen |> 
  group_by(dataset) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x, y, color = dataset)) +
  geom_point(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ dataset, ncol = 5) 
```

## 3D? Pie? How about 3D Pie? Ugh...

<br>

<center>![](https://external-preview.redd.it/perception-of-3d-pie-charts-v0-2ZPMqni3MhfCh4-8g_Lq1dj0yN-dT2CuC5VbeNn4aXg.jpg?auto=webp&s=5d6c7a6840094e58c6f43ddc67597ba49dac6038){width="800"}</center>

## Don't be misleading

<center>

<blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr"><a href="https://twitter.com/hashtag/CrookedHillary?src=hash&amp;ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">#CrookedHillary</a> = Obama&#39;s third term, which would be terrible news for our economic growth - seen below. <a href="https://t.co/y9WJoUaaql">pic.twitter.com/y9WJoUaaql</a></p>&mdash; Donald J. Trump (@realDonaldTrump) <a href="https://twitter.com/realDonaldTrump/status/759507812963737600?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">July 30, 2016</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script>

</center>

## The simpler the better

<center>![](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ryurko/SURE22-examples/main/figures/lecture_examples/nyt_ex.png){width="1400"}</center>

## [The grammar of graphics](https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/0-387-28695-0)

* Key idea: specify plotting "layers" and combine them to produce a graphic
* `ggplot2` provides an implementation of the grammar of graphics, with the following layers 

. . .

1. `data`: one or more datasets (in tidy tabular format)
2. `geom`: geometric objects to visually represent the data (e.g. points, lines, bars, etc.)
3. `aes`: mappings of variables to visual properties (i.e. aesthetics) of the geometric objects
4. `scale`: one scale for each variable displayed (e.g. axis limits, log scale, colors, etc.)
5. `facet`: similar subplots (i.e. facets) for subsets of the same data using a conditioning variable
6. `stat`: statistical transformations and summaries (e.g. identity, count, smooth, quantile, etc.)
7. `coord`: one or more coordinate systems (e.g. cartesian, polar, map projection)
8. `labs`: labels/guides for each variable and other parts of the plot (e.g. title, subtitle, caption, etc.)
9. `theme`: customization of plot layout (e.g. text size, alignment, legend position, etc.)

## A primer of ggplot2

```{r}
library(ggplot2)
```

<br>

Data: `mpg` (for more information, type `help(mpg)` or `?mpg`)

<br>

| Variable | Description                                      | Variable Type |
|----------|--------------------------------------------------|---------------|
| `hwy`    | fuel economy (mpg) for city highway driving      | quantitative  |
| `displ`  | engine displacement (liters)                     | quantitative  |
| `class`  | car type (compact, minivan, etc.)                | categorical   |

## Starting with the data.

```{r}
#| include: false
ggplot2::theme_set(ggplot2::theme_gray(base_size = 20))
```


::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
<br>

```{r}
#| eval: false
ggplot(data = mpg)
```

<br>

or equivalently, using `|>`

```{r}
#| eval: false
mpg |> 
  ggplot()
```

<br>

So far, nothing is displayed
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 9
ggplot(data = mpg)
```
:::
:::

## Specifying variables and geometric object

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| eval: false
mpg |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) +
  geom_point()
```

* Adding (`+`) a geometric layer of points to the plot

* Aesthetic mapping: `displ` to x-axis and `hwy` to y-axis via `aes()`

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 9
mpg |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) +
  geom_point()
```
:::
:::

## Adding additional variables

::: columns
::: {.column width="42%"}

```{r}
#| eval: false
mpg |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = displ, y = hwy, color = class)) +
  geom_point()
```

* To add additional variables to a plot, use other aesthetics like `color`, `shape`, etc.

* Give each point a unique `color` corresponding to its `class` (car type)

:::

::: {.column width="58%"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 9
mpg |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = displ, y = hwy, color = class)) +
  geom_point()
```
:::
:::

## Scaling mapped variables

Remember: one scale for each mapped variable

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%" style="text-align: left;"}
<br>

```{r}
#| eval: false
mpg |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = displ, y = hwy, color = class)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_x_reverse() + 
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 45, 15),
                     limits = c(0, 50)) +
  scale_color_discrete(palette = "Dark2")
```

* Flip the x-axis scale

* Change y-axis label breaks and limits

* Change color palette

:::

::: {.column width="50%" style="text-align: left;"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 9
mpg |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = displ, y = hwy, color = class)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_x_reverse() + 
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 45, 15),
                     limits = c(0, 50)) +
  scale_color_discrete(palette = "Dark2")
```
:::
:::

## Always label your plots! (seriously...)

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%" style="text-align: left;"}
<br>

```{r}
#| eval: false
mpg |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = displ, y = hwy, color = class)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_x_reverse() + 
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 45, 15),
                     limits = c(0, 50)) +
  scale_color_discrete(palette = "Dark2") +
  labs(
    x = "Engine displacement (liters)", 
    y = "Fuel economy (mpg)", 
    color = "Car type",
    title = "Highway fuel economy vs. engine displacement"
  )
```

* Each mapped aesthetic can be labeled
:::

::: {.column width="50%" style="text-align: left;"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 9
mpg |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = displ, y = hwy, color = class)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_x_reverse() + 
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 45, 15),
                     limits = c(0, 50)) +
  scale_color_discrete(palette = "Dark2") +
  labs(
    x = "Engine displacement (liters)", 
    y = "Fuel economy (mpg)", 
    color = "Car type",
    title = "Highway fuel economy vs. engine displacement"
  )
```
:::
:::

## Changing aesthetics without mapping variables

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
<br>

```{r}
#| eval: false
mpg |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  geom_point(color = "darkblue", size = 2, alpha = 0.4)
```

* Manually set the `color`, `size`, and `alpha` (transparency) of the point layer
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 9
mpg |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  geom_point(color = "darkblue", size = 3, alpha = 0.4)
```
:::
:::

## More customization

```{r}
#| eval: false
mpg |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) +
  geom_point(color = "darkblue", size = 2, alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") + # add smooth regression line
  scale_x_reverse() +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 45, 15), limits = c(0, 50)) +
  labs(x = "Engine displacement (liters)", 
       y = "Fuel economy (mpg)", 
       title = "Highway fuel economy vs. engine displacement") + 
  theme_light() + # change the theme
  # update text justification and font face
  theme(axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"))
```

## More customization

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 7
mpg |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) +
  geom_point(color = "darkblue", size = 2, alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") + # add smooth regression line
  scale_x_reverse() +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 45, 15), limits = c(0, 50)) +
  labs(x = "Engine displacement (liters)", 
       y = "Fuel economy (mpg)", 
       title = "Highway fuel economy vs. engine displacement") + 
  theme_light() + # change the theme
  # update text justification and font face
  theme(axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"))
```


## Faceting

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%" style="text-align: left;"}
```{r}
#| eval: false
mpg |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  facet_wrap(~ class)
```

* Create a multi-panel plot faceted by a conditioning variable (in our case, `class`)

* These facet/panel plots are sometimes called lattice plots or trellis plots

:::

::: {.column width="50%" style="text-align: left;"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 10
mpg |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  facet_wrap(~ class)
```
:::
:::
